1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	Stating the triple-overlap multiplicativity of the descent datum, however, first
46	requires a small piece of base-change machinery for the pullback functor on sheaves
47	of modules: the three transition isomorphisms attached to a triple of charts live a
48	priori on three different overlaps, and to compare their composite they must be
49	transported to a single common overlap. The next two blocks supply exactly this
50	transport -- the pseudofunctorial comparison of iterated pullbacks
51	(\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and its packaging along the triple-overlap data of a
52	scheme glue datum (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) -- after which the
53	cocycle condition can be expressed cleanly.
54	
55	\begin{definition}[Comparison of iterated pullbacks of sheaves of modules]
56	  \label{def:modules_pullbackComp}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}
58	  \mathlibok
59	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
60	  Let \(a : X \to Y\) and \(b : Y \to Z\) be morphisms of schemes. For a sheaf of
61	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\), pulling back first along \(b\) and then
62	  along \(a\) agrees with pulling back along the composite \(b \circ a\): there is a
63	  natural isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
64	  \[
65	    a^{*}\bigl(b^{*}\mathcal{M}\bigr)
66	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
67	    (b \circ a)^{*}\mathcal{M},
68	  \]
69	  natural in \(\mathcal{M}\), expressing that \(\mathcal{M} \mapsto \mathcal{M}\)'s
70	  pullback is a pseudofunctor on schemes. These comparison isomorphisms satisfy the
71	  pentagon (associativity) coherence for a triple of composable morphisms. This is the
72	  pullback half of the pseudofunctoriality of sheaves of modules and is supplied by
73	  Mathlib.
74	\end{definition}
75	
76	\begin{lemma}\leanok
77	[Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism to a triple overlap]
78	  \label{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
79	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackBaseChangeTransport}
80	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
81	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) with charts
82	  \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow
83	  U_i\), transitions \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), and triple-overlap
84	  data \(t'_{ijk}\) with its compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\). For an index
85	  triple \((i,j,k)\) write
86	  \[
87	    V_{ijk} \;=\; V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}
88	  \]
89	  for the common triple overlap, with the two projections
90	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) and \(p^{ik}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ik}\), and
91	  let \(t'_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{jki}\) be the glue datum's triple
92	  transition, satisfying
93	  \[
94	    t'_{ijk} \,\circ\, p^{jk}_{jki} \;=\; p^{ij}_{ijk} \,\circ\, t_{ij}
95	    \qquad (t_{\mathrm{fac}}).
96	  \]
97	  Given per-chart sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and a transition isomorphism
98	  \[
99	    g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
100	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
101	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
102	  \]
103	  on the overlap \(V_{ij}\) (as in \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}), the
104	  \emph{base-change transport} of \(g_{ij}\) to the triple overlap \(V_{ijk}\) is the
105	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{ijk}}\)-modules
106	  \[
107	    \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
108	      \;:\;
109	    (f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
110	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
111	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
112	  \]
113	  obtained by pulling \(g_{ij}\) back along \(p^{ij}_{ijk}\) and reassociating the
114	  iterated pullbacks via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. With the analogous transports
115	  \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) (the first taken along
116	  \(t'_{ijk}\), so that \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identifies its source and target sheaves
117	  with those of \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\)), all three
118	  transported isomorphisms become endomorphism-composable: \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\)
119	  and \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) land on a common sheaf, and their composite is
120	  comparable to \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) on \(V_{ijk}\).
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
125	  The pullback functor on sheaves of modules is a pseudofunctor: for composable
126	  scheme morphisms it carries the comparison isomorphisms of
127	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, natural in the sheaf and coherent for triple
128	  composites. Pulling the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\) back along the projection
129	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) yields an isomorphism between
130	  \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*} f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}(t_{ij} \circ
131	  f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\); applying \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} to each side
132	  reassociates these into the single-pullback form
133	  \((f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and
134	  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), giving
135	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\). For the \((j,k)\)-transition the same construction is
136	  applied along the triple transition \(t'_{ijk}\) rather than a bare projection; the
137	  glue datum's compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\),
138	  \(t'_{ijk} \circ p^{jk}_{jki} = p^{ij}_{ijk} \circ t_{ij}\), guarantees that the
139	  source of \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) coincides with the target of
140	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) (both are the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) along the same
141	  morphism \(V_{ijk} \to U_j\)), so the two are composable as endomorphism-style
142	  isomorphisms over \(V_{ijk}\). The reassociations introduced by
143	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} are coherent (they satisfy the pentagon identity), so
144	  the composite \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and the third
145	  transport \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are isomorphisms between the same pair of
146	  sheaves on \(V_{ijk}\), and may be compared directly. This is the module-level
147	  analogue of the scheme-level triangle \(t'_{ijk}\)/\(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) and is the
148	  shape in which the multiplicative cocycle condition is stated below.
149	\end{proof}
150	
151	\begin{lemma}[Triple-overlap endpoint bridges]
152	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
153	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_src,
154	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_mid,
155	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_tgt}
156	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
157	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) and fix
158	  indices \(i,j,k\) with triple overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\).
159	  The three transports of \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} have
160	  endpoints pinned down by three scheme-morphism equalities on \(V_{ijk}\), each a
161	  pure consequence of the glue datum's structural identities:
162	  \begin{itemize}
163	    \item (\emph{src}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ f_{ij} = \mathrm{snd} \circ f_{ik}\)
164	      -- the defining pullback condition of \(V_{ijk}\);
165	    \item (\emph{mid}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})
166	      = (t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ f_{jk}\) -- the pullback condition
167	      composed with the glue datum's \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identity;
168	    \item (\emph{tgt}) \((t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ (t_{jk} \circ f_{kj})
169	      = \mathrm{snd} \circ (t_{ik} \circ f_{ki})\) -- the cocycle core, from the
170	      \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) associativity, the reversed pullback condition, the
171	      \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\) collapse and the glue datum's cocycle identity.
172	  \end{itemize}
173	  These three equalities align the source, middle, and target sheaves of the three
174	  transports so that the cocycle equation (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}
175	  typechecks.
176	\end{lemma}
177	\begin{proof}
178	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
179	  Each equality is an identity of morphisms in the category of schemes, proved by
180	  rewriting with the glue datum's axioms: \emph{src} is literally
181	  \(\mathrm{pullback.condition}\); \emph{mid} adds the \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)
182	  factorisation \(D.t_{\mathrm{fac}}\,i\,j\,k\); \emph{tgt} chains
183	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at \((j,k,i)\), the reversed pullback
184	  condition, an auxiliary collapse combining \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at
185	  \((k,i,j)\) with \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\), and finally the cocycle identity
186	  \(D.\mathrm{cocycle}\,i\,j\,k\). No sheaf-of-modules data enters; these are
187	  bookkeeping equalities of the underlying scheme cover.
188	\end{proof}
189	
190	% NOTE (iter-056): the hand-built compatible-families presheaf route (this block +
191	% def:gr_modules_gluePresheafModule + lem:gr_modules_gluePresheaf_isSheaf) was ABANDONED.
192	% `Scheme.Modules.glue` is instead built directly as the equalizer of pushforwards
193	% `∏ᵢ (ιᵢ)_* Mᵢ ⇉ ∏_{ij} (j_ij)_* (f_ij^* Mᵢ)` (`X.Modules` HasLimits; pushforward+limits
194	% preserve the sheaf condition) — the compatible-families presheaf IS that equalizer. The
195	% `\lean{}` pins below name decls that DO NOT exist. Planner: rewrite the Construction prose
196	% of def:scheme_modules_glue to the equalizer route and drop these three phantom \uses edges.
197	\begin{definition}[The glued presheaf of modules: compatible families of chart sections]
198	  \label{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf}
199	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.gluePresheaf}
200	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso}
201	  With the descent data of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} -- a glue datum \(D\) with
202	  charts \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), immersions \(f_{ij}\) and transitions
203	  \(t_{ij}\), glued scheme \(X = D.\mathrm{glued}\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) with
204	  canonical immersions \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), per-chart sheaves
205	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) -- define, for each open
206	  \(V \subseteq X\), a \emph{compatible family} over \(V\) to be a tuple
207	  \((s_i)_{i \in J}\) with \(s_i \in \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i, \iota_i^{-1}V)\) for every
208	  \(i\), subject to the overlap relation: over each pairwise overlap \(V_{ij}\) the
209	  transition isomorphism \(g_{ij} : f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i \xrightarrow{\sim}
210	  (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\) carries the restriction of \(s_i\) (read as a
211	  section of \(f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) through the chart-restriction bridge
212	  \cref{lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso}) to the corresponding restriction of \(s_j\).
213	  The assignment
214	  \[
215	    V \;\longmapsto\; \bigl\{\,(s_i)_i \in \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i,
216	      \iota_i^{-1}V) \;\big|\; \text{overlap-compatible via } (g_{ij})\,\bigr\},
217	  \]
218	  with restriction maps acting componentwise (\(s_i \mapsto s_i|_{\iota_i^{-1}W}\) for
219	  \(W \subseteq V\)), is a presheaf on \(X\): componentwise restriction preserves the
220	  overlap relation because restriction is functorial and each \(g_{ij}\) is natural.
221	  This is the underlying presheaf of the glued sheaf.
222	\end{definition}
223	
224	\begin{definition}[The \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module structure on the glued presheaf]
225	  \label{def:gr_modules_gluePresheafModule}
226	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.gluePresheafModule}
227	  \uses{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf}
228	  The presheaf of \cref{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf} is a presheaf of
229	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules: over each open \(V \subseteq X\) the set of compatible
230	  families is an \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\)-submodule of the product
231	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i, \iota_i^{-1}V)\), where \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) acts on
232	  the \(i\)-th component through the restriction homomorphism
233	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(V) \to \mathcal{O}_{U_i}(\iota_i^{-1}V)\) induced by \(\iota_i\) and
234	  the \(\mathcal{M}_i\)-module structure. The overlap relation is preserved by addition
235	  and by scalar multiplication because each \(g_{ij}\) is \(\mathcal{O}\)-linear and the
236	  restriction-of-scalars maps commute with the chart immersions; the restriction maps
237	  of the presheaf are \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear for the same reason. This is the
238	  pointwise module structure assembled from the chart module structures.
239	\end{definition}
240	
241	\begin{lemma}[The glued presheaf is a sheaf]
242	  \label{lem:gr_modules_gluePresheaf_isSheaf}
243	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.gluePresheafIsSheaf}
244	  \uses{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf, def:gr_modules_gluePresheafModule,
245	    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib, def:gr_glued_scheme}
246	  The presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules of
247	  \cref{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf,def:gr_modules_gluePresheafModule} satisfies the
248	  sheaf condition: for any open \(V \subseteq X\) and any open cover
249	  \(\{V_\alpha\}\) of \(V\), a family of compatible families
250	  \((s^\alpha)_\alpha\) that agree on the pairwise intersections
251	  \(V_\alpha \cap V_\beta\) amalgamates to a unique compatible family over \(V\).
252	\end{lemma}
253	\begin{proof}
254	  \uses{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
255	  Gluing is checked componentwise. Fix a chart index \(i\); the \(i\)-th components
256	  \((s^\alpha_i)_\alpha\) form a compatible family of sections of the sheaf
257	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) over the cover \(\{\iota_i^{-1}V_\alpha\}\) of \(\iota_i^{-1}V\)
258	  (their agreement on overlaps follows by restricting the hypothesis to the chart), so
259	  by the sheaf gluing axiom for \(\mathcal{M}_i\)
260	  (\cref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) they amalgamate to a unique
261	  \(s_i \in \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i, \iota_i^{-1}V)\). The tuple \((s_i)_i\) is again
262	  overlap-compatible: the defining relation via \(g_{ij}\) is an equality of sections
263	  over \(V_{ij}\), it holds after restricting to each \(\iota_i^{-1}V_\alpha\)
264	  (where it is the compatibility of \(s^\alpha\)), and equality of sheaf sections is
265	  local, so it holds over \(V_{ij}\). Uniqueness of the amalgamation is the separation
266	  half of the same sheaf condition applied componentwise. Hence the glued presheaf is a
267	  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
268	\end{proof}
269	
270	\begin{definition}
271	
272	\leanok
273	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
274	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
275	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
276	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:modules_pullbackComp,
277	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges,
278	    def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf, def:gr_modules_gluePresheafModule,
279	    lem:gr_modules_gluePresheaf_isSheaf}
280	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
281	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
282	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
283	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
284	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
285	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
286	  charts. Suppose given:
287	  \begin{itemize}
288	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
289	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
290	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
291	      \[
292	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
293	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
294	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
295	      \]
296	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
297	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
298	  \end{itemize}
299	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
300	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
301	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
302	  \((g_{ij})\):
303	  \begin{enumerate}
304	    \item[(C1)] \emph{self-identity:} the self-transition is the identity,
305	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
306	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
307	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each common triple
308	      overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\) the base-change transports
309	      (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three transition
310	      isomorphisms satisfy
311	      \[
312	        \widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
313	          \;=\; \widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j},
314	      \]
315	      where \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\), \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\),
316	      \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are the transports of \(g_{ij}\), \(g_{jk}\),
317	      \(g_{ik}\) to \(V_{ijk}\) through the scheme-level triple transition
318	      \(t'_{ijk}\) and its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\), reassociated by the
319	      pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} so that all three land on
320	      the same sheaf -- the multiplicative, \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle
321	      condition.
322	  \end{enumerate}
323	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
324	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing is well-defined. Out of this
325	  data the construction produces a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
326	  \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\).
327	
328	  \emph{Construction.} The glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is assembled in three steps,
329	  each a separate sub-result: its underlying presheaf is the presheaf of compatible
330	  families of chart sections \cref{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf}; this presheaf carries
331	  a pointwise \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module structure \cref{def:gr_modules_gluePresheafModule};
332	  and it satisfies the sheaf condition \cref{lem:gr_modules_gluePresheaf_isSheaf}, the
333	  amalgamation being computed componentwise from the chart sheaf conditions. Packaging
334	  the resulting sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is the object \(\mathcal{M} =
335	  D.\mathrm{glued}.\mathrm{Modules}\) returned here. This specialises effective descent
336	  of sheaves of modules to the concrete open cover \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\)
337	  supplied by a scheme glue datum, and is the form in which the Grassmannian's
338	  tautological bundle and quotient are built.
339	
340	  The glued sheaf comes with three further structures, each recorded as its own block
341	  downstream: the \emph{restriction isomorphisms} \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}
342	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps
343	  \cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}; the \emph{characterisation up to unique
344	  isomorphism} by the restriction property \cref{lem:gr_modules_glue_unique}; and the
345	  \emph{descent of morphisms} -- a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\)
346	  commuting with the transition isomorphisms glues to a unique \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to
347	  \mathcal{N}\) with \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ
348	  \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\) \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom}.
349	\end{definition}
350	
351	\begin{definition}[Restriction isomorphisms of the glued sheaf]
352	  \label{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}
353	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestrictionIso}
354	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
355	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
356	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be the glued sheaf of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}. For each
357	  chart index \(i\) there is a \emph{restriction isomorphism} of sheaves of
358	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
359	  \[
360	    \rho_i \;:\; \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{M}_i,
361	  \]
362	  compatible with the transition isomorphisms: over each overlap \(V_{ij}\) the two
363	  restrictions \(\rho_i\) and \(\rho_j\), transported to the common overlap, differ
364	  exactly by \(g_{ij}\).
365	\end{definition}
366	\begin{proof}
367	  \uses{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
368	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
369	  By the compatible-family description \cref{def:gr_modules_gluePresheaf}, a section of
370	  \(\mathcal{M}\) over an open of the form \(\iota_i^{-1}V\), restricted to the chart
371	  \(U_i\), is recovered from and recovers its \(i\)-th component
372	  \(s_i \in \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i, \cdot)\); the chart-restriction bridge
373	  \cref{lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso} identifies the pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\)
374	  with this restriction, and the \(i\)-th projection \((s_\bullet) \mapsto s_i\) is the
375	  isomorphism \(\rho_i\) (with inverse extending a chart section by the overlap relation).
376	  The overlap compatibility is the defining relation of the compatible families: over
377	  \(V_{ij}\) the components \(s_i, s_j\) are related by \(g_{ij}\), which is precisely the
378	  statement that \(\rho_i\) and \(\rho_j\) differ by \(g_{ij}\) after transport to
379	  \(V_{ij}\); the triple-overlap coherence needed for this to be consistent is (C2),
380	  whose transports are aligned by \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} and
381	  \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}.
382	\end{proof}
383	
384	\begin{lemma}[Uniqueness of the glued sheaf up to canonical isomorphism]
385	  \label{lem:gr_modules_glue_unique}
386	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue_unique}
387	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
388	    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
389	  Suppose \(\mathcal{M}'\) is another sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules equipped with
390	  isomorphisms \(\rho'_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}' \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
391	  compatible with the \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps. Then there is a unique isomorphism
392	  \(\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}'\) intertwining the restriction
393	  isomorphisms, i.e.\ with \(\rho'_i \circ \iota_i^{*}(\cdot) = \rho_i\) for every \(i\).
394	\end{lemma}
395	\begin{proof}
396	  \uses{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
397	  A morphism of sheaves of modules on \(X\) is determined by, and may be assembled from,
398	  its restrictions to the members of the open cover \(\{\iota_i\}\), agreeing on the
399	  overlaps -- the locality and gluing of sections
400	  (\cref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) applied to the internal hom. The
401	  intertwining condition forces the restriction over \(U_i\) to be
402	  \((\rho'_i)^{-1} \circ \rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}'\);
403	  these local isomorphisms agree on each overlap \(V_{ij}\) because both
404	  \((\rho_i)\) and \((\rho'_i)\) are compatible with the \emph{same} transition family
405	  \((g_{ij})\) (\cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}). By locality they glue to a
406	  unique global isomorphism \(\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}'\) with the
407	  required property; uniqueness is the separation half of the same gluing.
408	\end{proof}
409	
410	\begin{definition}[Descent of morphisms along the cover]
411	  \label{def:gr_modules_glueHom}
412	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueHom}
413	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
414	    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
415	  Let \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) be two glued data
416	  over the same glue datum \(D\), with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\) and
417	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i, \rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i\). Given a family
418	  of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\)
419	  commuting with the transition isomorphisms on every overlap (i.e.\ \(h_{ij}\) intertwines
420	  the pullbacks of \(\phi_i\) and \(\phi_j\) over \(V_{ij}\)), there is a unique morphism
421	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) with
422	  \[
423	    \rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi \;=\; \phi_i \circ \rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i
424	    \qquad \text{for every } i.
425	  \]
426	\end{definition}
427	\begin{proof}
428	  \uses{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
429	  On each chart the prescribed identity defines a local morphism
430	  \((\rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i)^{-1} \circ \phi_i \circ \rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i :
431	  \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{N}\). The commutation of the \(\phi_i\)
432	  with the transition isomorphisms \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\), together with the overlap
433	  compatibility of the \(\rho\)-families (\cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}), makes
434	  these local morphisms agree on every overlap \(V_{ij}\). By the locality and gluing of
435	  sections (\cref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) applied to the internal hom they
436	  promote to a unique global morphism \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) restricting
437	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the
438	  cover.
439	\end{proof}

441	\subsection{Pullback of free sheaves along an arbitrary morphism}
442	\label{sec:grquot_pullback_free}
443	
444	The functor of points (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}) acts on morphisms by pullback, so
445	it needs the pullback of the trivial bundle to be again trivial --
446	\(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}\) -- for an
447	\emph{arbitrary} scheme morphism \(\varphi\). Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison applies
448	only when the underlying site functor \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is \emph{final}; the
449	following three blocks remove that restriction for every morphism, and the resulting
450	pullback machinery preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness.
451	
452	\begin{lemma}\leanok
453	[The opens-pullback functor is final]
454	  \label{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.opensMap_final}
456	  For any morphism of schemes \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the inverse-image functor on opens
457	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi : \mathrm{Opens}(T) \to \mathrm{Opens}(T')\),
458	  \(U \mapsto \varphi^{-1}U\), is \emph{final}.
459	\end{lemma}
460	
461	\begin{proof}
462	  Finality means that for every open \(V \subseteq T'\) the structured-arrow category of
463	  opens \(U \subseteq T\) with \(V \le \varphi^{-1}U\) is connected. That category is
464	  nonempty and has the terminal object \(U = \top\) -- one always has
465	  \(V \le \varphi^{-1}\top = T'\), and every object admits a unique arrow to \(\top\) --
466	  and a category with a terminal object is connected.
467	\end{proof}
468	
469	\begin{lemma}\leanok
470	[Pullback of a free sheaf is free]
471	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
472	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso}
473	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
474	  For any scheme morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), there is a canonical
475	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{T'}\)-modules
476	  \[
477	    \varphi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\bigr)
478	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
479	    \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}.
480	  \]
481	\end{lemma}
482	
483	\begin{proof}
484	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
485	  Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison produces this isomorphism whenever the site functor
486	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is final; that finality is \cref{lem:gr_opensMap_final},
487	  which holds for every morphism, so the comparison applies unconditionally.
488	\end{proof}
489	
490	\begin{lemma}\leanok
491	[Pullback preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness]
492	  \label{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
493	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
494	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
495	  Let \(\varphi : T' \to T\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) a sheaf of
496	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules locally free of rank \(d\)
497	  (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}). Then the pullback \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is
498	  locally free of rank \(d\) on \(T'\).
499	\end{lemma}
500	
501	\begin{proof}
502	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
503	  Choose an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(T\) trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\),
504	  \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\). The preimages
505	  \(\{\varphi^{-1}U_i\}\) cover \(T'\). On each member the factorisation
506	  \(\varphi \circ \iota_{\varphi^{-1}U_i} = (\varphi|_{U_i}) \circ \iota_{U_i}\) of the
507	  restricted morphism, together with the pseudofunctor comparison of iterated pullbacks
508	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), identifies
509	  \((\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M})|_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}\) with
510	  \((\varphi|_{U_i})^{*}\mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\); the latter is
511	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}^{\,d}\) by \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. Hence
512	  \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is locally free of rank \(d\).
513	\end{proof}
514	
515	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
516	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
517	
518	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
519	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
520	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
521	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
522	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
523	
524	\begin{definition}\leanok
525	[Global section of the unit module]
526	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
527	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
528	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
529	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
530	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
531	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
532	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
533	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
534	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
535	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
536	  global function.
537	\end{definition}
538	
539	\begin{definition}\leanok
540	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
541	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
542	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
543	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
544	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
545	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
546	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
547	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
548	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
549	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
550	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
551	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
552	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
553	\end{definition}
554	
555	\begin{lemma}\leanok
556	[Scalar endomorphism of the unit is the identity]
557	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}
558	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_one}
559	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
560	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the unit function \(1 \in
561	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the identity of the unit module:
562	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
563	\end{lemma}
564	
565	\begin{proof}
566	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
567	  Multiplication by \(1\) is the identity on every section, so under the
568	  identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the section \(1\)
569	  corresponds to \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
570	\end{proof}
571	
572	\begin{lemma}\leanok
573	[Scalar endomorphism of the zero function is zero]
574	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
575	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_zero}
576	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
577	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the zero function \(0 \in
578	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the zero endomorphism:
579	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\).
580	\end{lemma}
581	
582	\begin{proof}
583	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
584	  Multiplication by \(0\) sends every section to \(0\), so the corresponding
585	  endomorphism of the unit module is the zero map.
586	\end{proof}
587	
588	\begin{definition}
589	
590	\leanok
591	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
592	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
593	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
594	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
595	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
596	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
597	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
598	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
599	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
600	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
601	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
602	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
603	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
604	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
605	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
606	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
607	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
608	  \[
609	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
610	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
611	  \]
612	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
613	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
614	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
615	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
616	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
617	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
618	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
619	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
620	
621	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
622	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
623	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
624	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
625	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
626	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
627	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
628	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
629	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
630	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
631	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
632	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
633	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
634	  free sheaves'' primitive.
635	\end{definition}
636	
637	\begin{lemma}\leanok
638	[The $I$-columns of the chart quotient are the identity]
639	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
640	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_ιFree}
641	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
642	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
643	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
644	  For each \(k \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), the chart quotient \(u^I\)
645	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) carries the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th basis section of the
646	  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) -- where \(\iota_I(k)\) is the \(k\)-th
647	  element of \(I\) in increasing order -- to the \(k\)-th basis section of
648	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\); that is, the \(I\)-indexed columns of \(u^I\) form the
649	  identity:
650	  \[
651	    e_{\iota_I(k)} \,\circ\, u^I \;=\; e_k.
652	  \]
653	\end{lemma}
654	
655	\begin{proof}
656	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
657	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
658	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
659	  By the biproduct presentation of \(u^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}), the
660	  composite \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I\) reads off the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th column of the
661	  component matrix, whose \(p\)-th entry is the scalar endomorphism
662	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)})\). The \(I\)-indexed columns of the
663	  universal matrix \(X^I\), read through the order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong
664	  I\), form the \(d \times d\) identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}),
665	  so \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\). Hence the diagonal entry is
666	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the
667	  off-diagonal entries are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
668	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the resulting column is the \(k\)-th coordinate
669	  inclusion, giving \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I = e_k\).
670	\end{proof}
671	
672	\begin{lemma}\leanok
673	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
674	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
675	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
676	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
677	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
678	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
679	\end{lemma}
680	
681	\begin{proof}
682	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
683	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
684	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
685	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
686	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
687	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
688	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
689	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
690	  \[
691	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
692	  \]
693	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
694	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
695	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
696	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
697	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
698	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
699	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
700	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
701	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
702	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
703	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
704	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
705	\end{proof}
706	
707	\begin{definition}
708	
709	\leanok
710	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
711	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
712	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
713	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
714	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
715	    def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
716	    lem:gr_glueData_bridges, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
717	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
718	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
719	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
720	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
721	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
722	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
723	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
724	  %  by putting
725	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
726	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
727	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
728	  \[
729	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
730	  \]
731	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
732	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
733	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
734	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
735	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
736	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
737	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
738	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
739	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
740	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
741	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
742	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
743	\end{definition}
744	
745	\begin{definition}
746	
747	\leanok
748	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
749	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
751	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
752	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
753	    def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
754	    def:gr_modules_glueHom}
755	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
756	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
757	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
758	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
759	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
760	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
761	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
762	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
763	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
764	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
765	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
766	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
767	  \[
768	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
769	  \]
770	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
771	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
772	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
773	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
774	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
775	  \[
776	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
777	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
778	  \]
779	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
780	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
781	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
782	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
783	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
784	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
785	\end{definition}
786	
787	\section{The functor of points}
788	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
789	
790	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
791	rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
792	quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
793	functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
794	identities that make that action functorial.
795	
796	\begin{definition}[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
797	  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
798	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
799	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
800	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
801	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
802	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
803	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
804	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
805	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
806	  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
807	        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
808	  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
809	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
810	  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
811	  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
812	  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
813	  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
814	  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
815	  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
816	  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
817	  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
818	  functor's value at \(T\).
819	
820	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
821	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
822	  \[
823	    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
824	      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
825	        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
826	        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
827	        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
828	  \]
829	  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
830	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
831	  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
832	  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
833	  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
834	  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
835	  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
836	  map of equivalence classes.
837	\end{definition}
838	
839	The two blocks below record the comparison identities, between the free-pullback isomorphism
840	\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} and Mathlib's pseudofunctor structure on pullback, that make
841	the descended pullback action of \cref{def:gr_rankQuotient} functorial.
842	
843	\begin{lemma}\leanok
844	[Identity coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
845	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
846	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
847	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
848	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\), the canonical comparison morphism of unit
849	  modules \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) -- the map underlying the
850	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index -- coincides
851	  with the component at \(\mathbf{1}\) of the pullback-identity isomorphism
852	  \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
853	\end{lemma}
854	
855	\begin{proof}
856	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
857	  The comparison morphism is the mate of \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf 1}\) under the
858	  pullback--pushforward adjunction: the image of the identity of \(\mathbf 1\) through the
859	  adjunction's hom-equivalence on the unit. For the identity scheme morphism the pushforward
860	  is the identity functor, so the adjunction unit is itself an identity; Mathlib's
861	  pullback-identity isomorphism is exactly the conjugate (left-adjoint transpose) of the
862	  pushforward-identity isomorphism. Tracing the identity through the hom-equivalence and the
863	  conjugate-transpose identity for the pullback-identity isomorphism shows the comparison
864	  equals the \(\mathbf 1\)-component of that isomorphism.
865	\end{proof}
866	
867	\begin{lemma}\leanok
868	[Identity coherence at the free sheaf]
869	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}
870	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_id}
871	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
872	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\) and an index set \(I\), the free-pullback
873	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1) \cong \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) coincides
874	  with the component at the free sheaf \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) of the pullback-identity
875	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
876	\end{lemma}
877	
878	\begin{proof}
879	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
880	  Both sides are cocones on the coproduct \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\); by coproduct
881	  extensionality it suffices to check equality after precomposing with each coprojection
882	  \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element case
883	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}.
884	\end{proof}
885	
886	\begin{lemma}\leanok
887	[Composite coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
888	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
889	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
890	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
891	  For composable scheme morphisms \(a : T'' \to T'\) and \(b : T' \to T\), the comparison
892	  morphism of unit modules \((b \circ a)^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) factors as
893	  \(a^{*}\) of the comparison for \(b\), the comparison for \(a\), and the component at
894	  \(\mathbf{1}\) of the iterated-pullback comparison \(a^{*}b^{*} \cong (b\circ a)^{*}\)
895	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}).
896	\end{lemma}
897	
898	\begin{proof}
899	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
900	  This is the composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}: the mate
901	  construction is compatible with composition of adjunctions, so the comparison for
902	  \(b \circ a\) is the pasting of the comparisons for \(a\) and \(b\) reassociated by the
903	  pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The pentagon coherence of the
904	  iterated-pullback comparisons makes the factorisation well-defined.
905	\end{proof}
906	
907	\begin{lemma}\leanok
908	[Mate/conjugate compatibility of the adjunction hom-equivalence]
909	  \label{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
910	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
911	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2\) be two adjunctions and \(\alpha\) a natural
912	  transformation between their left adjoints. For a morphism \(f\) the hom-equivalence of
913	  \(\mathrm{adj}_2\) applied to \(\alpha_c \circ f\) equals the hom-equivalence of
914	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1\) applied to \(f\), post-composed with the component at \(d\) of the
915	  conjugate (mate) of \(\alpha\). This is the general naturality of the conjugate
916	  construction against the two hom-equivalences; it is the reusable engine behind
917	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}.
918	\end{lemma}
919	
920	\begin{proof}
921	  Both sides unfold to the same pasting of unit/counit triangles for the two adjunctions;
922	  the equality is the standard compatibility of the mate of \(\alpha\) with the
923	  hom-set bijections.
924	\end{proof}
925	
926	\begin{lemma}\leanok
927	[Composite coherence at the free sheaf]
928	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_comp}
929	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_comp}
930	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp, def:modules_pullbackComp}
931	  The composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}: for composable \(a, b\) and an

934	  the iterated-pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}.
935	\end{lemma}
936	
937	\begin{proof}
938	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
939	  By coproduct extensionality it suffices to check the identity after precomposing with each
940	  coprojection \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element
941	  composite coherence \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} (with the base-ring sheaf

945	Together \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
946	upgrade to the corresponding free-sheaf coherences -- the free-pullback isomorphism at the
947	identity equals the component of the pullback-identity isomorphism, and likewise for the
948	composite -- by coproduct extensionality over the decomposition
949	\(\mathcal{O}^{\,\oplus I} = \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\). These coherences discharge both
950	functoriality laws (identity and composition) of the Grassmannian
951	functor \cref{def:grassmannian_functor}.
952	
953	\begin{definition}
954	
955	\leanok
956	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
957	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
958	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
959	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
960	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
961	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
962	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
963	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
964	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
965	  % "represents the
966	  %  contravariant functor
967	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
968	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
969	  %  set of all equivalence classes
970	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
971	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
972	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
973	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
974	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
975	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
976	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
977	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
978	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
979	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
980	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
981	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
982	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
983	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
984	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
985	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
986	  \[
987	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
988	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
989	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
990	  \]
991	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
992	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
993	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
994	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
995	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
996	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
997	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
998	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
999	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
1000	\end{definition}
1001	
1002	\section{The universal property}
1003	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
1004	
1005	\begin{theorem}
1006	
1007	\leanok
1008	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
1009	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
1010	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
1011	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
1012	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
1013	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1014	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1015	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1016	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
1017	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1018	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
1019	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
1020	  %  contravariant functor
1021	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1022	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1023	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1024	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1025	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
1026	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
1027	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
1028	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1029	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
1030	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
1031	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
1032	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
1033	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
1034	  \[
1035	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
1036	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1037	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
1038	    \qquad
1039	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
1040	      \;\longmapsto\;
1041	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
1042	  \]
1043	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
1044	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1045	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
1046	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
1047	  universal family.
1048	\end{theorem}
1049	
1050	\begin{proof}
1051	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
1052	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
1053	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
1054	    def:grassmannian_functor}
1055	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
1056	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
1057	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
1058	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
1059	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
1060	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
1061	
1062	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
1063	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
1064	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
1065	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
1066	  the composite
1067	  \[
1068	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
1069	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1070	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
1071	  \]
1072	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
1073	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
1074	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
1075	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
1076	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
1077	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
1078	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
1079	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
1080	
1081	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
1082	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
1083	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
1084	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
1085	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
1086	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
1087	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
1088	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
1089	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
1090	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
1091	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
1092	
1093	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
1094	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
1095	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
1096	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
1097	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
1098	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
1099	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
1100	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
1101	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
1102	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
1103	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
1104	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
1105	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
1106	
1107	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
1108	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
1109	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
1110	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
1111	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
1112	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
1113	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
1114	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
1115	
1116	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
1117	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
1118	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1119	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1120	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChangeGlobal.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
5	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
6	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
11	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
12	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
13	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
14	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
15	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
16	out of scope here.
17	
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
19	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
21	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
22	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
23	\[
24	  \begin{array}{ccc}
25	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
26	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
27	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
28	  \end{array}
29	\]
30	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
31	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
32	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
33	
34	\section{The canonical base-change map}
35	
36	\begin{definition}\leanok
37	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
38	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
40	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
41	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
42	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
43	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
44	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
45	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
46	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
47	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
48	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
49	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
50	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
51	  change, I.}
52	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
53	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
54	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
55	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
56	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
57	  \[
58	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
59	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
60	  \]
61	  It is adjoint to the morphism
62	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
63	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
64	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
65	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
66	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\section{The affine case}
70	
71	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for quasi-coherent sheaf morphisms}
72	
73	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements

1	\chapter{Regrouping isomorphism for the affine base-change tensor tower}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_RegroupHelper}
3	% This chapter blueprints the standalone, geometry-free helper that lives in
4	% AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/RegroupHelper.lean (1:1 slug). It is the pure
5	% tensor-algebra core of lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv in the FlatBaseChange
6	% chapter; it is split into its own compilation unit so the object-level
7	% ModuleCat iso can be obtained by a single LinearEquiv.toModuleIso application
8	% (the separate import normalises the `Module A (A ⊗[R] R')` instance diamond
9	% that blocks the same-file construction).
10	
11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}
49	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
50	  \leanok
51	  This is pure tensor algebra, and it is supplied \emph{directly} by Mathlib's
52	  pushout-form base-change cancellation
53	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
54	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}), instantiated at \(S = R'\),
55	  \(B = A \otimes_R R'\):
56	  \[
57	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\,M :
58	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
59	  \]
60	  The required pushout instance \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,
61	  (A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\).
62	  The decisive structural point is that this equivalence is \emph{natively}
63	  \(R'\)-linear: the cancellation is bundled as a \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over
64	  \(R'\) (its base-ring slot is \(S = R'\)), so the \(R'\)-linearity is part of the
65	  Mathlib datum with no hand-written scalar-compatibility obligation.
66	  On the generator it computes, by \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\),
67	  \[
68	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
69	  \]
70	  with inverse \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
71	  (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)); the \(R'\)-action enters through the
72	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor (\(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)). Being a
73	  single bundled Mathlib linear equivalence it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and it
74	  carries no flatness hypothesis.
75	
76	\end{proof}
77

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
